source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 5.2 xargs command (pp. 72-74)

options: Builds and executes command lines using arguments read from standard input
Sorts standard input alphabetically
Compresses its standard input
Displays the arguments of a running process
questions.why: xargs reads whitespace/linefeed-delimited arguments from stdin and runs a command (echo by default) with them appended.

questions.q: If you run xargs with no command, which command does it run by default, and how do you end the input?
options: It runs echo; you end the input with Ctrl+D
It runs ls; you end the input with Ctrl+C
It runs cat; you end with Ctrl+Z
It runs nothing until you press Enter twice
questions.why: The default command is /bin/echo, and Ctrl+D signals end-of-input on stdin.

questions.q: Where does xargs place the arguments it reads from stdin, relative to the command?
options: At the END of the command line
At the very beginning, before the command name
In the middle, always after the first option
It replaces the command entirely
questions.why: xargs appends the input arguments at the end of the called command - which is why 'find ... | xargs tar cvf archive' works but cp (which wants the destination last) needs -I.

questions.q: Run 'ls -ld' once, taking the directories to list from standard input via xargs.
questions.why: xargs collects the stdin lines and runs 'ls -ld' with all of them as arguments in one go.

questions.q: What is the difference between the -L and -n options of xargs?
options: -L N processes at most N input LINES per command run; -n N passes at most N ARGUMENTS per run
-L sets the log level; -n names the command
-L N passes N arguments; -n N reads N lines
They are identical
questions.why: -L limits how many input lines feed each invocation; -n limits how many arguments per invocation - both force xargs to run the command multiple times.

questions.q: Copy each *.log file found under /var/log into /root/backup, using xargs with % as the placeholder for the input file so it is NOT put at the end.
questions.why: cp wants the destination last, so -I % marks where each input argument goes, letting the destination stay at the end.

questions.q: In 'xargs -I % cp % /root/backup', what does the % represent?
options: The placeholder where each input argument from xargs is substituted
A wildcard matching any file
The exit status of cp
A comment marker
questions.why: -I lets you choose a placeholder string (here %); xargs substitutes each input item at that position instead of appending it.
